subject: Rheumatology
title: Rheumatoid arthritis
preambleMarkdown:
# Rheumatoid arthritis



> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Rheumatoid arthritis is an infectious syndrome or organism-related topic in rheumatology.
- This article focuses on inflammation, autoimmunity, joints, systemic features, serology, imaging, and immunosuppression.
- The practical clinical priority is host-pathogen interaction, source control, and timely antimicrobial decisions.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Rheumatoid arthritis to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Rheumatoid arthritis is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is host-pathogen interaction, source control, and timely antimicrobial decisions.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Community-acquired, healthcare-associated, travel-related, device-related, or opportunistic infection.
- Localized, disseminated, recurrent, latent, or postinfectious presentation.
- Bacterial, viral, fungal, parasitic, toxin-mediated, or mixed infection.
- Uncomplicated disease, severe disease, sepsis, source-control need, or outbreak/public-health concern.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Rheumatoid arthritis depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In rheumatology, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Rheumatoid arthritis.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Rheumatoid arthritis disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Rheumatoid arthritis should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Rheumatoid arthritis first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Rheumatoid arthritis, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Fever, localizing symptoms, exposure history, immune status, device or procedure history, and severity define the syndrome.
- Immunocompromised patients, neonates, pregnancy, and older adults may lack classic symptoms.
- Sepsis, airway threat, neurologic involvement, necrosis, and rapidly progressive disease change urgency.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Rheumatoid arthritis, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused rheumatology examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Rheumatoid arthritis presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Rheumatoid arthritis with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Rheumatoid arthritis is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Rheumatoid arthritis should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize sepsis, obtain high-yield cultures when they will not delay care, and start empiric therapy when indicated.
- Narrow therapy once organism, susceptibility, source, and response are known.
- Add source control, isolation, prophylaxis, vaccination, contact tracing, or public-health notification when relevant.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Rheumatoid arthritis is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses host-pathogen interaction, source control, and timely antimicrobial decisions.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Start empiric antimicrobial therapy promptly when severe infection, sepsis, meningitis, necrotizing infection, or immunocompromised infection is suspected.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Drain abscesses, remove infected devices, debride necrotic tissue, decompress obstruction, or obtain diagnostic samples when source control is needed.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Rheumatoid arthritis, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Rheumatoid arthritis may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Rheumatoid arthritis patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Rheumatoid arthritis, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Rheumatoid arthritis in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Rheumatoid arthritis, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Rheumatoid arthritis does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Rheumatoid arthritis may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Rheumatoid arthritis, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Rheumatoid arthritis as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Do not delay antimicrobials in unstable sepsis.
- Cultures are most valuable before antibiotics when feasible, but stabilization comes first.
- Source control can matter as much as antimicrobial choice.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Rheumatoid arthritis draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current specialty society guidance, local protocols, and institutional pathways should be checked during clinical review.
- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.

### Source Excerpt: Rheumatoid Arthritis

_Source lines: 21993-22281._

```text
Rheumatoid Arthritis
A. General Characteristics
1. RA is a chronic inflammatory autoimmune disease involving the
synovium of joints. The inflamed synovium can cause damage to
cartilage and bone.

2. It is a systemic disease that has many extra-articular manifestations
(see below).

3. The usual age of onset is 20 to 40 years; it is more common in
women than in men (3:1).

4. Etiology is uncertain, but risk of RA is multifactorial. HLA-DRB1 is
the strongest genetic risk factor, and smoking is the strongest
environmental risk factor.

Quick HIT
Much of the joint damage that ultimately leads to disability occurs
early in disease, so early treatment with DMARDs is critical.

B. Clinical Features
1. Inflammatory polyarthritis that is most often a progressive disease,
though some patients may have intermittent symptoms without
severe progression (Figure 6-3)
a. Morning stiffness lasting >1 hour-improves as the day
progresses

b. Joints commonly involved include joints of the hands (PIP,
MCP) and wrists, knees, ankles, elbows, hips, and shoulders
c. Characteristic hand deformities
- Ulnar deviation of the MCP joints (Figure 6-4A)
- Boutonniere deformities of the PIP joints (PIP flexed, DIP
hyperextended) (Figure 6-4)
- Swan-neck contractures of the fingers (MCP flexed, PIP
hyperextended, DIP flexed) (Figure 6-4B)
2. Extra-articular manifestations (see Table 6-3)
a. Low-grade fever, weight loss
b. Fatigue can be prominent because this is a systemic disease
3. Cervical spine involvement is common at C1-C2 (subluxation and
instability), but it is less common in the lower cervical spine
a. Instability of the cervical spine is a potentially life-threatening
complication of RA. Most patients do not have neurologic
involvement, but if they do, it can be progressive and fatal if
not treated surgically.

b. This is seen in 30% to 40% of patients. All patients with RA
should have cervical spine radiographs before undergoing
any surgery (due to risk of neurologic injury during
intubation). However, disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs
(DMARDs) have dramatically reduced the need for cervical
spine surgery in RA patients.

4. Subcutaneous rheumatoid nodules over extensor surfaces may
also occur in visceral structures-for example, lungs, pleura,
pericardium (Figure 6-5)
a. Pathognomonic for RA
b. Nearly always occurs in seropositive patients (i.e., those with
RF)
Quick HIT
Poor Prognostic Indicators in RA
- High RF titers
- Subcutaneous nodules
- Erosive arthritis
Quick HIT
In RA, changes in joints are usually more extensive than in OA
because the entire synovium is involved in RA. Note that
osteophytes (characteristic of OA) are not present in RA.

FIGURE
6-3 The clinical course of RA can be grouped into four classes of patients.

FIGURE
6-4 Ulnar deviation of the MCP joints.

(A reprinted with permission from Harris JH, Harris WH, Novelline RA. The Radiology of
Emergency Medicine. 3rd ed. Williams & Wilkins; 1993:440. B reprinted with permission from
Smeltzer SC, Bare BG. Brunner and Suddarth's Textbook of Medical-Surgical Nursing. 10th
ed. Lippincott Williams & Wilkins; 2003. Figure 54-4B.)

TABLE 6-3 Extra-articular Manifestations in Rheumatoid
Arthritis
Constitutional
Symptoms
Malaise, Anorexia, Some Weight Loss, Fever
Cutaneous 	- Skin becomes thin and atrophic and bruises easily
- Vasculitic changes/ulcerations involving fingers, nail
folds
- Subcutaneous rheumatoid nodules (elbows, sacrum,
occiput)-pathognomonic for RA
Pulmonary 	- Pleural effusions (very common)-pleural fluid
characteristically has very low glucose and low
complement
- Pulmonary fibrosis-with a restrictive pattern on
pulmonary function tests and a honeycomb pattern on
CXR
- Pulmonary infiltrates
- Rheumatic nodules in lungs (similar to those on skin)
-can cavitate or become infected

Cardiac 	- Rheumatic nodules in heart-can lead to conduction
disturbances (heart block and bundle branch block)
- Pericarditis-in 40% of patients with RA
- Pericardial effusion
Eyes 	- Scleritis
- Scleromalacia-softening of the sclera; if not treated
may perforate, leading to blindness
- Dry eyes (and dry mucous membranes in general);
may develop Sjogren syndrome
Nervous System 	Mononeuritis multiplex-infarction of nerve trunk. Implies
systemic vasculitis, which is a bad sign
Felty Syndrome 	- Triad of RA, neutropenia, and splenomegaly
- Also anemia, thrombocytopenia, and
lymphadenopathy
- Associated with high titers of RF and extra-articular
disease
- Increased susceptibility to infection
- Usually occurs fairly late in the disease process
Blood 	- Anemia of chronic disease: Mild, normocytic,
normochromic anemia
- Thrombocytosis
Vasculitis 	A microvascular vasculitis-can progress to mesenteric
vasculitis, PAN, or other vascular syndromes
C. Diagnosis
1. The 2010 ACR/EULAR criteria are based on joint involvement,
positive serologies and elevated inflammatory markers, and
chronicity

## 2. Serology

a. Rheumatoid factor (RF) is sensitive but not specific for RA. RF is
eventually present in 80% of patients with RA (may be absent
early in the disease), but is also present in up to 3% of the
healthy population
- RF titers rarely change with disease activity and are not
useful for following patients.

- Helpful in determining prognosis. High titers -> more severe
disease
d. Anticitrullinated peptide/protein antibodies (ACPA)-sensitivity
is 50% to 75%, specificity is over 90%
Quick HIT

Patients with diagnosis of RA who have a positive RF, ACPA, or
both are at a higher risk of developing erosive joint damage-early
treatment with DMARDs is indicated.

1. Radiographs (Figure 6-6). Not required for a diagnosis of RA but
may have the following characteristic findings
a. Loss of juxta-articular bone mass (periarticular osteoporosis)
near the finger joints
b. Narrowing of the joint space (due to thinning of the articular
cartilage) is usually seen late in the disease
c. Bony erosions at the margins of the joint
2. Synovial fluid analysis (see Table 6-4)
FIGURE
6-5 Rheumatoid nodules of the hand.

(Reprinted with permission from Stedman TL. Stedman's Medical Dictionary for the Health
Professions and Nursing: Illustrated. 6th ed. Lippincott Williams & Wilkins; 2007.)

FIGURE
6-6 Posteroanterior radiographs of the hand showing the typical pattern of
involvement for (A) osteoarthritis (osteophytes, subchondral sclerosis, joint
space narrowing) (arrows) and (B) rheumatoid arthritis (periarticular
erosions, osteopenia) (arrows).

(A and B reprinted with permission from Humes HD, DuPont HL, Gardner LB, et al. Kelley's
Textbook of Internal Medicine. 4th ed. Lippincott Williams & Wilkins; 2000:1463. Figures
190.4A and B.)

TABLE 6-4 Synovial Fluid Analysis

Quick HIT
Variants of RA
- Felty syndrome: anemia, neutropenia, splenomegaly, and RA.

- Juvenile RA: begins before 18 years of age. Extra-articular
manifestations may predominate (Still disease) or arthritis may
predominate.

- Caplan syndrome: RA associated with pneumoconiosis.

Quick HIT
Combination therapy with first-line drugs (methotrexate,
hydroxychloroquine, and sulfasalazine) produces higher remission
rates.

Quick HIT
Radiographs have become less important in diagnosis of RA, as
joint abnormalities occur very late. DMARDs started well before
radiographs indicate abnormality.

Quick HIT
Methotrexate is the initial treatment for RA.

D. Treatment

1. Goal is to minimize pain and swelling, prevent disease progression,
and help patient remain as functional as possible
2. Exercise helps to maintain range of motion and muscle strength
3. Symptomatic treatment
a. NSAIDs are the drugs of choice for pain control
b. Corticosteroids (low dose)-use these if NSAIDs do not provide
adequate relief. Short-term treatment may be appropriate but
avoid long-term use

## 4. DMARDs

a. General principles
- Can reduce morbidity and mortality (by nearly 30%)-by
limiting complications, slowing progression of disease, and
preserving joint function
- Should be initiated early (at the time of diagnosis)
- They have a slow onset of action (6 weeks or longer for
effect to be seen), so begin treating RA while waiting for the
disease-modifying therapy to take effect. Gradually taper
and discontinue NSAIDs and corticosteroids once effects are
evident
e. Methotrexate-best initial DMARD
- Initial improvement is seen in 4 to 6 weeks
- Side effects include GI upset, oral ulcers (stomatitis), mild
alopecia, bone marrow suppression (reduced with folic
acid supplementation), hepatocellular injury, and
idiosyncratic interstitial pneumonitis, which may lead to
pulmonary fibrosis. It increases liver enzymes in some
patients
- Closely monitor liver and renal function
i. Alternatives: leflunomide, sulfasalazine, hydroxychloroquine,
TNF inhibitors
j. Non-TNF biologics (baricitinibb, tofacitinib, others) are also
options if disease activity remains high despite methotrexate
5. Surgery (in severe cases)
a. Synovectomy (arthroscopic) decreases joint pain and swelling
but does not prevent x-ray progression and does not improve
joint range of motion
b. Joint replacement surgery for severe pain unresponsive to
conservative measures
A 39-year-old woman with a history of rheumatoid arthritis presents for
routine care. The patient's symptoms are well controlled on her current
medication regimen. Routine laboratory values reveal the following.

Hemoglobin 	9.8 g/dL
Mean corpuscular volume (MCV) 	110 fL
Leukocyte count 	9,500/mm 3
Platelets 	280,000/mm 3
Which of the following is this patient taking for her rheumatoid arthritis?

A. Methotrexate
B. Cyclosporine
C. Corticosteroids
D. Hydroxychloroquine
- The answer is A: Methotrexate. The patient in this question has
rheumatoid arthritis and is presenting with a macrocytic anemia
(MCV >100 fL). Of the four choices, only methotrexate can cause a
macrocytic anemia. Methotrexate is a disease-modifying
antirheumatic drug (DMARD), which inhibits the metabolism of folic
acid by inhibiting dihydrofolate reductase. Other important side
effects include stomatitis, lung fibrosis, rash, and pancytopenia.

Therefore, patients on methotrexate need to have their blood counts
(CBC) checked every 3 months. (B) Cyclosporine is an
immunosuppressant drug used commonly after organ transplantation.

It is associated with increased viral infection, nephrotoxicity,
hypomagnesemia, and hepatotoxicity. (C) Corticosteroids are used in
the treatment of rheumatoid arthritis (RA) and side effects include
osteoporosis, impaired wound healing, and other effects seen in
Cushing syndrome. (D) Hydroxychloroquine is also a DMARD used in
the treatment of RA, but its side effects include visual problems and
hemolytic anemia found in G6PD deficiency.
```
